name: Aswang
description: The Aswang is a shapeshifting creature in Filipino folklore, often described as a combination of a vampire, a witch, and a werebeast. It is a nocturnal creature that preys on humans, particularly infants and pregnant women. Aswangs are believed to have the ability to separate their upper torso from their lower body, flying off to hunt while leaving the lower half behind. They are often depicted as having a long, proboscis-like tongue used to suck out the fetus from a pregnant woman's womb or to extract the organs of sleeping victims. They are said to be able to transform into animals like dogs, cats, pigs, or birds to move undetected. In some versions, they are daytime humans who lead normal lives, concealing their true nature. The Aswang's motives are often attributed to hunger or a desire to spread their evil lineage. They are one of the most feared creatures in Filipino mythology, and their existence is still believed in by many Filipinos, especially in rural areas. They are said to be repelled by garlic, salt, and religious objects.
subtitle: Shapeshifting monster of Filipino folklore
appearance: Aswangs can appear as normal humans during the day, often described as shy or withdrawn. At night, they can transform into various animals, most commonly dogs, cats, pigs, or large birds. When in their hunting form, they are often described as having long, black hair, sharp claws, and glowing red eyes. Some accounts describe them with a long, proboscis-like tongue used for feeding. When the upper torso detaches, it is said to be accompanied by the sound of flapping wings.
habitat: Aswangs are said to inhabit rural areas, particularly in remote villages and forests. They are often associated with abandoned houses, cemeteries, and other places considered to be haunted or unlucky. They typically live among humans, concealing their true nature until nightfall.
behavior: Aswangs are primarily nocturnal hunters, preying on humans, particularly infants and pregnant women. They are said to be cunning and deceptive, often using their shapeshifting abilities to lure victims into traps. They are known to imitate the voices of loved ones to gain entry into homes. Aswangs are also believed to spread diseases and cause misfortune. They are driven by hunger and a desire to perpetuate their kind.
weaknesses: Sharp objects (especially if used to sever the tongue)

keyMyths.title: The Flying Torso
keyMyths.description: One of the most common myths is the Aswang's ability to separate its upper torso from its lower body and fly, searching for prey. The sound of 'tik-tik' indicates the Aswang is near, with louder sounds meaning it is further away.

keyMyths.title: Pregnancy and Infants
keyMyths.description: Aswangs are particularly drawn to pregnant women and infants, seeking to suck out the fetus or organs.  This is why pregnant women often wear garlic necklaces for protection.

sources.author: Maximo Ramos
sources.work: The Creatures of Philippine Lower Mythology
sources.description: A comprehensive study of various creatures in Filipino folklore.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of various creatures in Filipino folklore.

sources.author: Jordan Clark
sources.description: A collection of stories about various Filipino mythological creatures, including the Aswang.
